text: Average weekly running distance, number of weekly runs, maximum weekly distance, number of runs ≥32km, average training pace, longest run distance, and weekly running hours all have a statistically significant negative association with marathon finish time, meaning increases in each parameter coincide with faster finishes.
Average weekly running distance explains a significant proportion of variance in marathon performance and is a key training determinant for marathon finish time.
The number of runs of 32km or longer completed during the pre-marathon training block is significantly associated with faster marathon finish times.
Average running pace during training is significantly associated with marathon performance, with faster training paces predicting faster finish times.
The rate of non-finishers in marathon races is approximately 7.27%.
Training volume metrics (weekly distance, weekly hours, maximum weekly distance) show a dose-response relationship with marathon performance, where higher volumes predict faster finish times.
Training frequency (number of weekly runs) is independently associated with faster marathon performance.
